behaviors.description: Cron parser rejects invalid expressions with descriptive error messages
behaviors.justification: Parsing is pure validation logic—no I/O, dependencies, or randomness. Invalid input (month=13, day=32, malformed syntax) produces a deterministic error. A mock cannot hide a broken parser; if the validator doesn't correctly identify invalid input or wrongly rejects valid input, the test catches it immediately.
behaviors.test_description: Unit test that passes invalid cron expressions (month=13, day=32, too many/few fields) and verifies each raises ValidationError with a message identifying the specific invalid field. Also verify that edge-case valid expressions (e.g., '0 0 L * *' for last day of month) parse successfully.

behaviors.description: Next-run-time calculator returns correct timestamps for standard cron and edge cases
behaviors.justification: Computing next N run times from a fixed start time is deterministic calendar arithmetic—no I/O, no OS calls, no randomness. A failure (wrong timestamp, off-by-one month, incorrect 'last day' or 'nearest weekday' logic) manifests as a simple value mismatch. Mocking cannot hide this; any logic error is immediately visible in the result.
behaviors.test_description: Unit test that calls the calculator with fixed start times (e.g., May 15, 2024 10:00 AM UTC) and cron expressions, verifying returned run times match expected values. Include edge cases: last day of month (cron field 'L'), nearest weekday ('W'), runs skipping months, and runs very close to the start time. Verify both timestamp values and (if applicable) timezone correctness.

behaviors.description: Overlap detector identifies schedules that conflict within minimum gap threshold
behaviors.justification: Overlap detection is pure comparison logic—given two candidate-run-time lists and a gap threshold, find conflicts. This is entirely in-process and deterministic. A failure (missing overlap, false positive, wrong conflict window, mishandled boundary conditions) manifests as wrong detection result. Mocking the schedule objects does not hide errors in the detection algorithm itself.
behaviors.test_description: Unit test that creates two schedule objects (from known cron expressions) and calls the overlap detector with a minimum gap (e.g., 5 minutes). Verify correct identification of: (a) schedules that fire within the gap (returns overlap with correct time window), (b) schedules that fire beyond the gap (returns no overlap), and (c) boundary cases (e.g., one ends at minute 0, the other starts at minute 5—does gap=5 allow this?).

behaviors.description: Timezone-aware scheduling produces correct run times across DST transitions
behaviors.justification: Correct DST handling can be verified using real timezone objects (e.g., `zoneinfo.ZoneInfo`) and fixed, known transition dates (e.g., US/Eastern March 10 spring-forward, November 3 fall-back), without relying on system time or current tzdata. A failure (ambiguous time, non-existent run skipped or not, wrong offset) manifests as a wrong timestamp. Using real timezone objects ensures the test catches logic errors; mocking the timezone library would defeat the purpose.
behaviors.test_description: Unit test that calculates next run times for a job scheduled at 2:30 AM in a DST-observing timezone, using fixed dates spanning spring-forward and fall-back transitions (e.g., US/Eastern March 8–10, November 1–3). Verify: (a) during spring-forward (2:00–3:00 AM doesn't exist), the engine skips or advances the run per its configured behavior; (b) during fall-back (2:00–3:00 AM occurs twice), run times are unambiguous and properly ordered; (c) results are stable across re-runs and don't depend on the current system date.

behaviors.description: Missed-run policy correctly skips or backfills when next run time has passed
behaviors.justification: Policy application is deterministic logic—given a missed run time and a policy enum, decide whether to skip or backfill. This can be tested entirely in-process by passing a synthetic 'current time' parameter (or mocking `datetime.now()` if the engine reads it) to avoid reliance on system clock. A failure (wrong policy applied, skipped when should backfill) is immediately visible in the output decision.
behaviors.test_description: Unit test that simulates a scenario where the calculated next run time has passed (e.g., current time is 10:00 AM, next run was 9:00 AM). Verify the policy logic: (a) if policy is 'skip', the next run advances to the following occurrence; (b) if policy is 'backfill', the engine returns/marks the missed run for execution; (c) edge cases like multiple consecutive missed runs are handled per policy (e.g., skip all, backfill recent N). Use fixed 'current time' values to avoid system-clock dependence.
